Untrusted user-supplied data is inserted into a context that can execute JavaScript.
the parameter 'result' is tainted
Untrusted user-supplied data is inserted into a context that can execute JavaScript.

The untrusted data reaches a sink that can either lead to HTML injection, JavaScript code execution, or the manipulation of a URL starting with the "javascript:" or "data:" schemes. Any of these can lead to a DOM XSS vulnerability. HTML injection: Either escape properly the untrusted data or use a safe API to insert this data to the DOM; direct HTML manipulation as text should be avoided. JavaScript code execution: Validate any untrusted data against an allowlist so it is not possible for an attacker to have its supplied code executing. URL manipulation: Add trusted schemes to an allowlist and ensure it does not allow for the injection of a URL like: `data:text/html;,<img/src/onerror=alert(1)>`.

The untrusted data reaches a sink that can either lead to HTML injection, JavaScript code execution, or the manipulation of a URL starting with the "javascript:" or "data:" schemes. Any of these can lead to a DOM XSS vulnerability.
* HTML injection: Either escape properly the untrusted data or use a safe API to insert this data to the DOM; direct HTML manipulation as text should be avoided.

* URL manipulation: Make sure the scheme is whitelisted and doesn't allow for the injection of a URL like: "data:text/html;,<img/src/onerror=alert(1)>".

A string intended to be matched literally is instead treated as a regular expression, changing its meaning.
The "." character in the pattern ""org.owasp.webgoat.lessons."" can match any character, because calls to "replaceAll" treat the pattern as a regular expression, which might be unexpected.
To replace all occurrences of the literal string, use "replace" instead of "replaceAll".

The application uses the `io.jsonwebtoken.JwtParser` package's `parse()`, `parsePlaintextJwt()`, or `parseClaimsJwt()` method to parse a JWT without first verifying the token's signature, allowing an attacker to forge the token claims and perform attacks such as impersonating other users.

The application uses the `io.jsonwebtoken.JwtParser` package's `parse()`, `parsePlaintextJwt()`, or `parseClaimsJwt()` method to parse a JWT without first verifying the token's signature, allowing an attacker to forge the token claims and perform attacks such as impersonating other users.
Modify the application to use `parseClaimsJws()` or `parsePlaintextJws()` instead, since these methods will automatically verify the token signature.

The untrusted data reaches a sink that can either lead to HTML injection, JavaScript code execution, or the manipulation of a URL starting with the "javascript:" or "data:" schemes. Any of these can lead to a DOM XSS vulnerability.
* HTML injection: Either escape properly the untrusted data or use a safe API to insert this data to the DOM; direct HTML manipulation as text should be avoided.
* JavaScript code execution: Validate any untrusted data against a whitelist so it's not possible for an attacker to have its supplied code executing.
* URL manipulation: Make sure the scheme is whitelisted and doesn't allow for the injection of a URL like: "data:text/html;,<img/src/onerror=alert(1)>".
Parameter "result" is tainted when the callback function is called.

A user-controllable string is used as part or all of a filesystem path, filename, or URI (uniform resource identifier).
"importFile" is an entry point function.
Calling "getOriginalFilename". Reading data from an HTTP request, which is considered tainted. (The virtual call resolves to "org.springframework.web.multipart.MultipartFile.getOriginalFilename()".)

Calling "resolve". This call assigns "multipartFile.getOriginalFilename()" to "<return value>". Now "<return value>" is tainted. (The virtual call resolves to "java.nio.file.Path.resolve(java.lang.String)".)
Assigning: "destinationFile" = "destinationDir.toPath().resolve(multipartFile.getOriginalFilename())".
Passing "destinationFile" to "deleteIfExists".
Constructing a path or URI using the tainted value in "destinationFile". This may allow an attacker to access, modify, or test the existence of critical or sensitive files.

Path manipulation vulnerabilities can be addressed by proper input validation. Disallowing directory traversal characters (using a deny list) can improve the safety of the input, but the recommended approach is to restrict to a specific set of allowed characters (using an allow list). This should exclude absolute paths and upward directory traversal.

The instance of `JwtBuilder` doesn't set the expiration time when generating the token. Ignoring the expiration time for a JSON web token means it is valid forever. Since the token never expires, an attacker has a longer window to brute-force or exploit the stolen the token.

The instance of `JwtBuilder` doesn't set the expiration time when generating the token. Ignoring the expiration time for a JSON web token means it is valid forever. Since the token never expires, an attacker has a longer window to brute-force or exploit the stolen the token.

The application returns a `Set-Cookie` HTTP response header without the `SameSite` attribute. As a result, the client will use the default value which, in older browsers, is the insecure value `None`. In this case, the client will send the cookie for cross-origin requests, making Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks possible if other mitigating controls are absent.

The application returns a `Set-Cookie` HTTP response header without the `SameSite` attribute. As a result, the client will use the default value which, in older browsers, is the insecure value `None`. In this case, the client will send the cookie for cross-origin requests, making Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks possible if other mitigating controls are absent.
Set the `Samesite` attribute on this cookie to a safe value such as `Strict` or `Lax`.

Hashing a password using a computationally cheap cryptographic hash function. An attacker can compute more hashes per second with a cheaper function than with a more expensive function; therefore, if such password hashes leak to an attacker, they can recover more passwords in less time.

The recommended method of hashing sensitive password data is to generate a random sequence of bytes (a "salt") for each password to hash, hash the password and the salt with an adaptive hash function such as bcrypt, scrypt, and PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2), and then store the hash and the salt for subsequent password checks.

The application returns a `Set-Cookie` HTTP response header without the `SameSite` attribute. As a result, the client will use the default value which, in older browsers, is the insecure value `None`. In this case, the client will send the cookie for cross-origin requests, making Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks possible if other mitigating controls are absent.

Path manipulation vulnerabilities can be addressed by proper input validation. This should exclude absolute paths and upward directory traversal.

The `secure` attribute is not enabled for cookies.
While web browsers send cookies with the `secure` attribute only over HTTPS, they do send cookies without it over HTTP.
If an attacker can fool the victim's browser into sending an HTTP request to the vulnerable site (for example, with JavaScript code on a malicious page or with a phishing link), the victim's browser will send the vulnerable cookie in clear text.
If the attacker can also intercept this network traffic, then they can steal the victim's session cookie and impersonate the victim.

The `secure` attribute is not enabled for cookies.
While web browsers send cookies with the `secure` attribute only over HTTPS, they do send cookies without it over HTTP.
If an attacker can fool the victim's browser into sending an HTTP request to the vulnerable site (for example, with JavaScript code on a malicious page or with a phishing link), the victim's browser will send the vulnerable cookie in clear text.
If the attacker can also intercept this network traffic, then they can steal the victim's session cookie and impersonate the victim.
Enable the `secure` attribute by calling `Cookie.setSecure(true)` to prevent the cookies from being sent over an insecure channel.

Untrusted user-supplied data is inserted into an apparent SQL statement without adequate validation, escaping, or filtering.
"attack" is an entry point function.
The "userId" is tainted.
"userId" is concatenated to a string that appears to form a SQL query.

Use one of the following methods:
* Use a query-preparation API to safely construct the SQL query containing user-supplied values.
* Only concatenate a user-supplied value into a query if it has been checked against a whitelist of safe string values, or if it must be a Boolean or numeric type.
